speaker: interviewer
text: Elisabeth, you were just telling me at the end of the other tape about the way the ghetto was structured. Please continue with that.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. We were in Hungary. And very, very suddenly hap-- happened everything. First, the occupation announced in 19-- March 19. Then about 25th, I think, March 25th, the yellow star and the yellow armband came to effect-- armband for the soldiers and stars for the civil-- civilians. And everything was going too fast. The SS men came to the people's door, knocked, preferable at night when you were asleep, and asking for the head of the family, beating him senseless to confess where are the fortune of the Jews. And between the time, you had to donate your typewriter, radio, and bicycle-- because that's what we had, what was a lifeline for us. And my father, every night about midnight or so, was listening to the radio, to the BBC before the occupation. That's the British Broadcasting Company. And that gave us some hope. Because somehow, we knew what was happening outside of Hitler's territory. But after the, the radio was taken away, we had no way to know what's going on. Curfew came in. We were allowed to go out between 12:00 or-- and 2:00, or something like that, to take care of errands. What you couldn't do because when I was standing out in line for, for milk or bread, for example, when I came to the door with my yellow star, they said everything is gone. But the person who stood behind me got all the food, what she was asking for.

speaker: interviewee
text: So right away, that was horrible things. When they told us we ca-- we have to go the ghetto, appointing the Jews' homes as ghetto, they said we can take everything what we wanted to take, what we can carry in our two hands. So you cannot carry too man-- too many things.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto, we were-- at least in the place where I was-- unfortunately together with Christian people who did not know they are counting as Jew because one of their grandparents were Jewish. And when they did find out they have to come to the ghetto, they became very, very angry at us, Jews in the ghetto, instead of thinking about that Hitler's order, not the Jews' order.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we suffered in the ghetto even from them. The SS men came in every single day-- two courageous guy, never a single one-- and again and again ask for jewelry and money and everything, beat the people to confess things where they are hiding something, and telling us that we will be relocated. And if we are willing to work, everything will be wonderful. Because in the ghetto in two bedroom and a kitchen, 50 people were put, strange people who didn't know each other. So that was a horrible, crowded place with one outhouse. Where did you sleep when you were in that room with all those people?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the floor. You had no place else. There were no furniture, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pillows and blankets that was feather, you know. Can't remember the name. You have it-- no blanket, but that thick cover what you put on the bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: The duvet or comforter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Comforter, yes-- that's what we took. And that's-- that's was our bed. And you was laying next to someone whom you never knew before. Anyway, the situation was terrible. Many people suicided. And as a punishment, they didn't take the bodies out, but left it middle of the yard. The yard was fenced around. And the gate was locked all the time, what is even today so in Hungary. And the Hungarian policemen stood before the door. I wanted to mention to you that during my time in concentration camp and so on, my opinion about the German was horrible. Because they were unbelievable, the atrocities what they were capable to do. But I wanted to tell you between the German people were also some decent people-- not many, unfortunately. Because if that would have been many, that wouldn't have happened what happened. And that goes for the Hungarians also-- my beloved Hungarians, you know, whom I trusted so much. And I was ready to give my life for Hungary. They were many, many, many of them, most of them, who were pointing out the homes of the Jews and didn't offer any help.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we did go to the ghetto. My father had two workers. He, he had four, but two who came to our door and said we should give everything what we have to them, because we will be killed anyway. So-- and they were Hungarians that worked for my father many, many, many years. And my parents helped them with everything beside their paycheck. So that was happening to us. What was unbelievable, we still tried to deny why things happening the way happening. In the ghetto, the SS men one day came in and said we have 10 minutes to pack together everything what we want to take. And we will be relocated. We are going to the railroad station. So whatever we could grab, we did. We did go middle of the road in the city. And my beloved Hungarians were lined in the sidewalks looking at us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And many people asked for water. Because between us were all the people, sick people, kicked out from the hospital because they were Jewish. And many young mothers carrying their babies and young children, asking for water. And nobody offered, not even one drop of water. And I saw some of my school mates who were singing in our house together with us. And when I looked at them, they turned their head away. And I felt so horrible that I felt something broke inside in me. And I never, ever could forget or forgive them. When we came to the railroad station, they put 100 people into the railroad cars. And they were all the Jews, not only from my city, but the surrounding smaller villages Jews had to come into to make the transport. And 100 people settled into the cattle car. We sat on our folded legs, because we had no place to stretch out, and so close to each other you couldn't move your hand or arms. They shut the door. That was a horrible, horrible sound when they chained it. We had no windows and no benches, nothing, in that cattle car, and no toilet, water, or food. Then finally, after hours waiting, the train started to roll. We didn't know where we are going or how long we will go. The heat already inside in the car was unbearable. And the stench, the screaming, the crying of the children and the babies, the begging of the sick people for medication or water-- that was unbearable. But we had no way to do anything for each other. There were people who were praying. There were people who were cursing the praying people. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that's the way we did go, four and a half days. After two days, the noise level a little bit was lower. Because all the babies died. But then you heard the mother screaming and beating the child, trying to make them alive again. And the sick people died.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the third and fourth day, you started to feel the stench of the decomposing body. Because inside of the cattle car, the heat was unbelievable hot. I am sure that was 130 or even more degree.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, after four and a half days' ride, we arrived somewhere. And the train stopped. And the door opened up. I remember when the door opened up, I felt such happiness, such a relief. But that took just one second and every feeling gone because I looked into a pitch dark emptiness full of tortures, two SS men standing by the railroad car and two striped uniformed so-called [INAUDIBLE], whom I didn't know who these guys were. Later, I learned they were Jewish young men chosen by the SS men to do the most horrible things on Earth to do to their own people. They were telling us to jump from the train. We couldn't even get up. And finally, the people-- we, 2,000 people-- got off from the train, except the dead, naturally. They told us, [INAUDIBLE], we should leave the packages. Everything will follow us. And when we all came off from the train, the noise was deafening. People were screaming and crying, calling each other. I remember so clearly how I holded onto my mother and my little brother and my father and the other aunt, making a ring together, desperately trying to keep together.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the loudspeaker came on and told us to separate, the men should come form a group. And women and children should form another group. [CRYING] And then my little brother unfortunately turned to me and asked me, where do I belong-- to the men or to the women? Because in the Jewish religion, when you reach age 13, you become a bar mitzvah. What does that mean? That you study the Bible, and when you are 13 years old, you go to the temple. And before God and before the congregation, you make an oath that from now on, you are responsible for your deed, whatever you do. And you count as a man. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my little brother just five months earlier became bar mitzvah. So he didn't know where he does belong. Unfortunately, I told him to go with my mother. Because mothers are the people who take care of sick children. And my brother was very, very sick during the four and a half days' trip. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Unfortunately, I didn't know that with my advice, I killed my brother. [CRYING] Because all the children and all the mothers were taken directly to the gas chamber. [CRYING] I didn't know that. I didn't know where we are to begin with. But I never could forgive myself. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: So as I was standing with my mother and my little brother, I looked to on my father. And my father was a six foot, two inch tall, very strong man. He was standing there so miserable. And the tears was running on his face. I never saw my father crying like that. So I didn't even think a moment. I started to run toward him. I wanted to tell him something, comfort him. I don't know what I wanted. Maybe I just wanted to hug him. When halfway through, an SS man stepped forward. And with a rubber stick what they were always carrying with them, started to beat me and scream at me, go back where you belongs. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, I didn't know that we arrived to Auschwitz-Birkenau. I didn't know a Jew is not allowed to speak to a German. So I started to beg him, please let me go to my father, just for one moment-- please, just one second. And he got even angrier, never, never stopped the beating. I felt all over my face and my back, the blood running, and told me again, go back where you belongs. You will see your father tomorrow morning. And I remember so clearly that I couldn't move. I was frozen. I just didn't move. And he was not stopping the beating. I don't know how long I was standing there-- a minute, a second, two minute. I have no idea. Finally, I realized that I have to go back to my mother and my little brother. And when I did, they were nowhere. They disappeared. And then I felt a tremendous push behind my back. I didn't even hear when the loudspeaker told us to separate all the single women from the children and mothers and form a third group and walk forward. But that's what happened. So I was pushed. And I think about, I don't know, 50 yard or, or more, they were standing, an SS man, a young man, and waving left and right. I didn't know what does that mean. When I came face to face with him, I didn't feel any fear. He was smiling. He was very relaxed. I didn't know what left meant, or right. Later, I learned left meant going directly to the gas chamber. And right meant you are saved for a short time till you are able to work. And if you are not, then you are shot or sent to the crematorium, gas chamber. Or you just died from hunger or torture or just whatever. That man was Dr. Josef Mengele. He was nicknamed by the inmates the Angel of Death. And he was the angel of death. I met him several more times on selection. But in that time, I didn't know anything what's going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after the initial selection?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- excuse me. When I was showed to go to right, then they told us to take off our clothes and fold it very neatly, because we will get it back. And then we were, were taken into a big room, where they shaved us, all the hair off our body and, and head, and put into an even larger room where-- that was full of pipes. I looked up. I couldn't move, because so many people were pushed into that room. Finally, the water came. Later, I learned that was also the gas chamber. And with one twist, I could turn the water or the gas on. When we came out from that chamber, we had to wait for everybody came out. We got a piece of cloth. I got a man's shirt, had no button on it. And I felt terrible, ashamed, humiliated. I felt naked. I was naked. And I looked around. Everybody was bald. I didn't realize I was also bald. And that was about 2 o'clock in the morning. We arrived Friday night. And that-- as I was waiting for the rest of the group, I looked toward-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know what direction, where we were guided. There were a big fire, like a bonfire, like a Christmas tree-- always comes back to me like that, and little babies' faces twisted in pain, looking at me, and little arms and legs hanging out of the flame. And then-- because many of the children were thrown into the flame, grabbed from the mother's arm and thrown into the flame. But naturally, we didn't know. We didn't know where we are. And then some of the girls around me started to scream that they are burning us alive. And then we realized that the stench, what we feels, is the burning flesh of human, and the fat of human, and so on. That's what happened in the first night. When we came to the barracks, the girls started to-- I, I didn't speak for three weeks, not one word, after that scene.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when the girls came into the barracks where we were sent, they were screaming and crying, where are our parents and sisters and brothers? And one of the SS men walked up and with the rubber stick pointed to the sky and said, look up. What do you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: And what we have seen, very heavy black smoke covering the whole sky. And he was laughing. He said, there are your sisters and parents, and there where you are going. And he turned around and walked away, laughing. Even today, many times I hear the laugh. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That was my first day in Auschwitz-Birkenau. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I was the only one from my whole family. Even I did go into with a lot of uncles and aunts, cousins, and nephews the very same night. I was the only survivor. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was five months in Auschwitz. Because after two weeks, asking questions, you know, groups who were passing us as we were going somewhere, where we were taken somewhere to work, asking groups passing each other if they know where is my sister, asking my sister's name. And I found-- I did find out that she was in the E camp. I wanted desperately to meet her. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] finally, I found out that the camp where I was, that was 3B, Hungarian camp. One side of the camp-- you know, all the camps with the barracks were surrounded with electrified wires, very tall-- I don't know, maybe nine feet tall. I don't know exactly how tall. But one side of our, our camp wasn't. Because they never finished the camp. They never intended us to survive. We were not even numbered. Because the time we came in, we were the last transp-- not la-- not the very last from Hungary, but the last country taken the people to camp were directed, directed to a gas chamber. What did they do or why they decided not to put us all in the oven, I don't know. But we did go to that camp what was not finished. And a few blocks-- I mean, a few yard, maybe 100 yard or so far, or even farther, in that side what never was finished, was three other barracks. And that was some kind of [INAUDIBLE] called. I did find out that my sister is going there to work. But that took many, many weeks after I was in Auschwitz and hiding behind my girlfriend's back in roll calls to stay there to be able to see my sister. And finally, when I did see her, I over to her. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We hugged. And she said, we have to survive and tell the world what happening here. Because no one would ever believe that and told me, please eat everything and go back. That took just a few second. And I did run back. Between the working barracks and our barracks were no fence but an SS man with a German Shepherd. You know, they were patrolling also the camp. But by that side, there were the SS man with the dog. When I came back, my girlfriends told me that when  the SS saw I am running toward my sister, he told to the dog to go after me. And the dog didn't move. And when I came back, the dog and the SS man still were frozen. I don't know what happened. But the SS man never said anything to me. I was never punished for that, what I would have gotten to be shot. Because that was a different place. That was the last time I saw my sister. Many years later of liberation, I learned that she was shot at-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] lined up every 10 people-- was shot at Bergen-- in Bergen-Belsen by the SS men three days before the Eng-- English soldiers liberated Bergen-Belsen. And that's the way she died.
